description: Forward-looking roadmap for OMC. Each item carries effort estimate, priority, prerequisites, and rationale. Status moves left-to-right: exploratory → planned → in_progress → shipped. Items in the `shipped` section are kept for context — what got us here informs what's next.

vision.tagline: A small language with first-class φ-math, embedded Python, and a real package manager.
differentiators: Self-hosting compiler with self-healing built into the math substrate (not a hand-written rule table)
Embedded CPython always-on — every pip-installable library is reachable from OMC
Multi-dim harmonic anomaly detection that demonstrably beats IsolationForest on structural anomalies
Two-engine architecture (tree-walk + bytecode VM) with byte-identical output
Package manager + registry + sha256 verification, comparable to npm/cargo for a single-author research project
audience: Researchers exploring substrate-based language design (φ-math as static analysis)
Engineers needing structural-anomaly detection on multi-dim telemetry (drop-in IsolationForest replacement)
Data scientists who want OMC's harmonic primitives layered on top of pandas/numpy/sklearn
Language hackers studying self-hosting + self-healing as a coupled system

effort_levels.large: 1-2 sessions, multi-file refactor or new subsystem
effort_levels.research: open-ended, may not converge in a single session

priority_levels.1: Critical path / blocker for downstream work

phases.name: Shipped
phases.summary: What's already in the repo on origin/master. Listed for context — useful to grep against when planning what builds on what.

items.title: Self-hosting compiler fixpoint (gen2 == gen3)
Five-class self-healing pass (harmonic, typo, /0, arity, parser)
Rc<RefCell> Value::Array/Dict — killed n² collection cliff
Embedded CPython always-on (py_import / py_call / py_eval / etc.)
Bidirectional Python↔OMC callbacks (py_callback)
omc --install + omc.toml + omc_modules/ + sha256 verification
Central registry at registry/index.json (np, pd, sk, requests, sqlite, torch, harmonic_anomaly)
harmonic_anomaly library — beats IF 10/10 vs 7/10 on credential-stuffing
match expression with attractor ranges, type tags, alternation
Mini Scheme interpreter in OMC (closures, recursion, quote, let)
JSON parser+serializer in OMC (recursive descent, mutable-closure cursor)
Stack traces with source line numbers, both engines

phases.name: Next (1-3 sessions)
phases.summary: Items I'd pick up immediately. Each one extends an existing strength rather than opening a new direction. Strong cost/value ratio.

items.title: harmonic_clustering library — drop-in KMeans replacement
items.rationale: harmonic_partition naturally produces clusters keyed by Fibonacci attractor. Wrap as ha_clusters.fit(rows) → cluster_assignments. Comparison target: KMeans (deterministic, no random_state). Likely wins on attractor-aligned data, ties on uniform-random — same shape as harmonic_anomaly's win story.

items.title: harmonic_recommend library — CF-style recommendation
items.rationale: MovieLens demo already showed harmonic_index works for recommendations. Package as: ha_rec.fit(user_item_ratings); ha_rec.suggest(user, top_k). Comparison target: surprise/scikit-learn-recommender. Real adoption story for the harmonic_index primitive.

items.title: omc --test as standalone CLI mode
items.rationale: test_runner.omc is already library-shaped. Wrap as a CLI subcommand: `omc --test FILE` runs every fn test_*() and reports. Crucial for treating OMC as a real engineering tool, not just a research toy.

items.title: omc --bench as standalone CLI mode
items.rationale: examples/benchmarks.omc has the right shape. CLI wrapper that runs `fn bench_*()` and reports per-op ns. Useful for performance regression CI.

items.title: Multi-dim harmonic detection on more datasets
items.rationale: Credential stuffing is one demo. Build at least 3 more: insider exfiltration (large download + off-hours + new device), API abuse (high request rate + uncommon endpoints + valid auth), DDoS pattern detection. Each one a standalone demo that proves the multi-dim story generalizes.

items.title: elif as a parser-level shorthand for `else { if ... }`
items.rationale: OMC has Statement::If with elif_parts already in the AST. Just need parser to accept `elif COND { ... }` as syntactic sugar. Removes the ugliest pattern in user code (deeply nested else-if chains in JSON parser, Lisp eval).

phases.name: Strategic (3-10 sessions)
phases.summary: Larger architectural moves. Each one unlocks a new capability tier.

items.title: LSP server for IDE integration
items.rationale: VS Code / nvim plugin is the difference between 'curious researchers try it' and 'engineers use it'. Token-level positions are already in the parser; the heal pass can become a code-action provider; type inference can drive completion. tower-lsp crate makes this tractable.
items.deliverable: omnimcode-lsp/ crate + VS Code extension scaffolding

items.title: WASM target for browser/Node/edge deployment
items.rationale: Two paths: (a) compile omnimcode-core to wasm32 via wasm-bindgen, ship to npm. (b) compile OMC bytecode to WASM directly (longer-term). Path (a) gets the embedded interpreter into browsers in days. Pyo3 won't work in the browser; ship a `--no-default-features` WASM build alongside the desktop binary.

items.title: CUSUM-based time-aware anomaly detection
items.rationale: Phase B+1 documented honestly that simple top-K detectors (us included) hit a wall at NAB. Real wins need CUSUM (cumulative sum change-point detection), seasonality decomposition via FFT, or HMM/LSTM autoencoders. CUSUM with harmonic flavoring is the most tractable. Goal: beat IsolationForest on NAB realKnownCause windows-covered metric. Currently tied 7/19 each.

items.title: Real central registry server (PR-able, versioned, hosted)
items.rationale: Currently registry/index.json lives in the repo and updates require PR-and-merge. Real registry (a tiny Rocket/axum service backed by JSON files in a registry repo) supports: semver pinning, multiple versions per package, search, download counts. Modeled on cargo registry protocol.

items.title: Source positions on every AST node, not just Call
items.rationale: Stack traces include line numbers but only for call sites. Errors INSIDE a function body show the function's call site, not the failing line. To fix: thread Pos through every Statement and Expression variant. Mechanical but voluminous (~80 sites). Unblocks proper debugger story.

items.title: async/await primitives + concurrent runtime
items.rationale: Real network/IO programs need concurrency. Three options: (a) green threads via the interpreter, (b) tokio integration via pyo3 (every py_call returns a Future), (c) defer to Python's asyncio entirely. (c) is cheapest, (a) is most OMC-native. Significant design work.

items.title: JIT compilation via cranelift
items.rationale: Bytecode VM is ~2× tree-walk. JIT could be 10-100× on hot loops. cranelift is the right toolchain (used by wasmtime). Big architectural change; likely worth it once language stabilises and there's a real workload demanding it.

phases.name: Research (open-ended)
phases.summary: Directions where the answer might be 'not worth it' and that's still a valid result. High variance, high upside.

items.title: no_std variant of omnimcode-core for kernel embedding
items.rationale: User's stated long-term interest: 'micro/macro crossbreed → OS path'. Carve out a minimal no_std core (no Python, no file I/O, no regex), put behind a feature flag. Kernel modules / embedded firmware could embed it. Unclear if anyone actually needs this, but the architecture supports it now (Rc-shared collections work in no_std with a custom alloc).

items.title: Audio / music integration — literally harmonic primitives
items.rationale: OMC's φ-math is 'harmonic' in the abstract sense (Fibonacci attractors). Audio is harmonic in the literal sense (frequency ratios). py_import('librosa') gives us spectrograms; harmonic_index could fingerprint songs by attractor distribution; key/chord detection without ML training. Speculative but the math actually fits.

items.title: ECG arrhythmia detection on MIT-BIH dataset
items.rationale: Heartbeats have natural attractor structure (RR intervals cluster on patient resting rate). Modern approaches use deep learning trained on labeled data; classical use threshold rules. Harmonic_index on RR-interval folds could surface arrhythmias as off-attractor beats without training. PhysioNet MIT-BIH database is public. If it works: 'lightweight on-device anomaly detection without trained models' — directly applicable to smartwatches.

items.title: Attractor-based compression for scientific data
items.rationale: Data that naturally clusters on attractors (sensor readings discretised to calibration steps, RPM as multiples of 60, prices ending in .99) compresses worse than it should under gzip — entropy coding doesn't know about attractor structure. fold-then-delta encoding could beat gzip on this class. Niche but novel.

items.title: Distributed harmonic computing — CRDT-style attractor merging
items.rationale: Speculative. fold(x) is associative-commutative on the same value: fold(fold(x)) == fold(x). This is exactly the property CRDTs need. A harmonic_set could be a CRDT — multi-node merging by attractor key produces convergent results. Far-future architecture for distributed anomaly detection.

phases.name: Ecosystem & adoption
phases.summary: Not technical work — community, documentation, real users. Critical for the project to outlive its single-author phase.

items.title: One real team using harmonic_anomaly in production
items.rationale: The library beats IsolationForest on a benchmark. Next question is whether it beats it on someone's actual production telemetry. Find an SRE team running ML-driven alerting and offer to A/B their existing IF setup against harmonic_anomaly. Real-world signal is the hardest credibility check.

items.title: Multi-part tutorial series (blog/video/docs)
items.rationale: README is dense. New users need a path: 'hello world → first script → first harmonic primitive → first integration → first contribution'. Five tutorials total, each ~30 min. Bonus: each tutorial doubles as a regression-tested OMC file.

items.title: Position paper: 'φ-math as a static analysis substrate'
items.rationale: Self-hosting + self-healing + harmonic anomaly detection wins (10/10 vs 7/10) is enough material for a workshop paper at a PL or ML systems conference. Frame: language design where the static analysis substrate is mathematics rather than types. Cite NAB results, harmonic_anomaly comparison, and the Fibonacci-attractor primitive set.

items.title: Talk at PL or systems conference
items.rationale: Rust LATAM, Strange Loop (RIP), HYTRADBOI, RustConf — pick one, submit. Demos are concrete (live REPL doing harmonic anomaly detection on Wikimedia API logs); architecture story is novel (φ-math substrate); package manager makes the language plausibly real. Workshop submission window is shorter than full paper.

items.title: Discord / Matrix / discussion forum
items.rationale: Not yet — single-developer project doesn't need a chat room. Defer until there's >5 contributors or >1 real deployment. Premature community infrastructure is worse than none.

phases.name: Known issues & cleanup
phases.summary: Real bugs and rough edges from PAIN_POINTS.md and accumulated session notes. Smaller items, mechanical fixes.

items.title: OMC_HEAL respects literal context (no auto-rewriting domain values)
items.rationale: From PAIN_POINTS MED-3: heal pass rewrites `target = 4.0` (rating=4 stars) to `target = 3` because 4 isn't Fibonacci. Need @no_heal decoration support OR position-aware heuristic that only fires on indices/array positions. Currently heal pass is unsafe-by-default for any program with domain semantics on small ints.

items.title: Selective imports (`from "path" import name1, name2`)
items.rationale: Currently `import "path" as alias;` imports the entire module. Selective imports avoid namespace pollution and make multi-file programs cleaner. Mechanical parser + interpreter change.

items.title: Float display always shows decimal point
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS LOW-1: `println(3.0)` prints `3` (Rust's default float display). Adds int-vs-float ambiguity to user output. Fix: format as `{:?}` or special-case integer-valued floats.

items.title: omc --audit mode (runs both engines, diffs output)
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS LOW-2: when float-truncation bug landed, both engines silently produced different wrong answers. An audit CLI mode that runs both and flags divergence catches this class of bug instantly. Trivial to implement; reuses the regression sweep machinery.

items.title: csv_parse(text) builtin for fast CSV ingestion
items.rationale: PAIN_POINTS HIGH-2: load_csv is 28ms for 10k rows post-Rc-shared (was 9.9s). Most of remaining time is per-line str_split round trips through VM dispatch. A native csv_parse would bring 10k load to <5ms. Less critical now that Rc-shared closed the gap, but the long tail is still slow.

items.title: Native VM lowering for match expression (currently tree-walk fallback)
items.rationale: match compiles to Op::ExecStmt (tree-walk fallback) right now. Works correctly but loses the bytecode-VM speedup for match-heavy code (e.g. the Lisp evaluator). Native lowering would emit guarded jump tables. Straightforward, ~50 lines per pattern variant.

items.title: Native VM lowering for try/catch (currently tree-walk fallback)
items.rationale: Same as match — try/catch compiles to Op::ExecStmt. Native lowering needs a side try-stack and Result-aware op dispatch. Bigger refactor than match lowering. Defer until try/catch shows up in a hot path (it doesn't yet).

items.title: Confirm old GitHub PAT is revoked (user action only)
items.rationale: Local cleanup done in this session: token stripped from .git/config, ~/.git-credentials deleted, gh auth set up via OAuth token in keychain. The OLD PAT (ghp_1zz...425YytI) was visible in this conversation transcript and any earlier git remote -v outputs. User must manually revoke at https://github.com/settings/tokens.
items.deliverable: user clicks 'Delete' on the old token in GitHub settings

principles: Honest results over flashy ones — document negative findings (NAB tied 7/19, harmonic features hurt Titanic survival). Negative results published cleanly are credibility, not loss.
Real datasets find real bugs — toy demos missed the float-truncation bug for months; one MovieLens run flushed it in 5 minutes.
Integration scripts get written in OMC, not Rust — np.omc is 30 lines on top of py_*. Users fork integrations without Rust knowledge.
Two-engine parity is non-negotiable — every feature lands in tree-walk and bytecode VM with byte-identical output.
Don't build features that no one needs yet — async, JIT, OS embedding all stay 'exploratory' until a real workload pulls on them.
anti_goals: Becoming Python — Python wins on every axis except harmonic semantics. Don't compete with it; embed it.
Building features for hypothetical users — every shipped library has a measurable win story or it doesn't ship.
Premature ecosystem infrastructure — Discord/forum/CoC machinery before there are 5 contributors is dead weight.
Optimization for synthetic benchmarks — the only benchmarks that matter are real workloads (MovieLens, NAB, credential stuffing), not microbenches we tuned for.
